summary: The case reveals modifiable predictors of lateness and negative self-evaluation: time-keeping behavior, underestimated task durations, pre-departure organization, lack of buffers, variability in routine durations, and harsh self-evaluation. A multi-domain plan targeting these levers with cognitive-behavioral, environmental, and bio-physiological adjustments can reduce lateness while improving mood and self-efficacy. The proposed solutions emphasize (1) habit formation with buffers and cues, (2) cognitive recalibration of task durations, (3) organizational supports for departure readiness, (4) compassionate self-talk to reduce self-criticism, and (5) sleep and self-monitoring enhancements to sustain changes over 4–8 weeks, with simple feasibility and outcome metrics.

solutions.solution_candidate: Schedule a fixed 15-minute buffer before every departure/commitment, and deploy two reminders (30 minutes prior and 5 minutes prior) plus a simple pre-departure checklist (keys, phone, bag) with a one-tap confirmation to start.
solutions.mechanism_rationale: Buffers absorb unpredicted delays and reduce decision fatigue at departure; reminders reinforce planned start times; checklists lower cognitive load and misplacement risk, collectively reducing lateness frequency and duration variability.
solutions.measurement: Average buffer minutes per departure; proportion of on-time arrivals; pre-departure checklist completion rate; task start-time accuracy.
solutions.mapping_query: How does adding a fixed 15-minute buffer and reminders before commitments influence lateness frequency, start times, and duration variability (C01/C04/C05) in a typical week?
